parts.prompt:
Identify one technological development in communication or transportation in the twentieth
century.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for identifying ONE technological development in communication or transportation in the 20th century. CB-acceptable responses: (1) the development of the internet; (2) the airplane; (3) cellular communications. Note: some technologies that originated in the late 19th century but were widely developed or commercialized in the early 20th century (e.g., radio, automobile) can be credited.

sample_responses.response: The development of the internet — from the ARPANET in the late 1960s to the consumer World Wide Web in the 1990s — was the defining communication technology of the 20th century, allowing near-instantaneous global exchange of text, data, and later audio and video.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a specific, CB-listed 20th-century communication technology and briefly situates its development. CB-aligned.

sample_responses.response: The commercial airplane — from the first sustained powered flight by the Wright brothers (1903) through mass jet travel after the Boeing 707 (1958) — was a transformative 20th-century transportation development.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a CB-listed transportation development and briefly specifies its 20th-century trajectory. CB-aligned.
Does not identify any specific technological development.

required_elements: The development of the internet was a major development in communication.
alternatives.correct_example: The development of the internet was a major development in communication.

required_elements: During the twentieth century, one important technological development was the airplane.
alternatives.correct_example: During the twentieth century, one important technological development was the airplane.

required_elements: The development of cellular communications was a life-changing development. Scoring Note: Some technologies that originated in the late nineteenth century but were further developed, commercialized, and/or became widespread in the early twentieth century can be credited toward Part A. Examples include the radio and the automobile.
alternatives.correct_example: The development of cellular communications was a life-changing development. Scoring Note: Some technologies that originated in the late nineteenth century but were further developed, commercialized, and/or became widespread in the early twentieth century can be credited toward Part A. Examples include the radio and the automobile.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Restating the prompt without providing a defensible response.
Providing an example not relevant to the specific period or region asked.
common_traps: Identifies a person or event without explaining its historical relevance.
Provides multiple examples when only one is needed — focus on quality not quantity.

parts.prompt:
Explain one way nuclear technologies affected international relations in the second half of
the twentieth century.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for explaining ONE way nuclear technologies affected international relations in the second half of the 20th century. CB-acceptable responses: (1) invention of nuclear weapons led some countries to pursue them and drove international nonproliferation efforts; (2) the threat of nuclear war (Mutually Assured Destruction) kept the superpowers at peace despite their rivalry; (3) the impossibility of direct nuclear war pushed the US and USSR into proxy conflicts around the world.

sample_responses.response: Nuclear weapons shaped international relations through Mutually Assured Destruction (MAD): because any direct superpower war would be annihilating for both sides, the US and USSR avoided open military conflict with each other for the entire Cold War, instead channeling their rivalry through diplomatic standoffs (the Cuban Missile Crisis of 1962) and arms-control negotiations (SALT I, 1972).
sample_responses.commentary: Explains a CB-listed effect (MAD keeping superpowers at peace) with specific historical events. Historically accurate and well-connected.

sample_responses.response: Because nuclear war between the US and USSR would have been catastrophic for both, the superpowers instead fought each other indirectly through proxy wars in Korea, Vietnam, Afghanistan, Angola, and elsewhere — nuclear weapons thus channeled Cold War competition into regional conflicts rather than direct confrontation.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains a CB-listed effect (proxy war channeling) with multiple accurate examples. CB-aligned.
Does not explain a specific effect on international relations.

required_elements: The invention of nuclear weapons complicated international relations as some countries sought [and sometimes acquired] nuclear weapons, which resulted in international efforts to limit proliferation.
alternatives.correct_example: The invention of nuclear weapons complicated international relations as some countries sought [and sometimes acquired] nuclear weapons, which resulted in international efforts to limit proliferation.

required_elements: The threat of nuclear war kept the major superpowers at peace because they both believed in the MAD doctrine and did everything possible to avoid direct war between themselves.
alternatives.correct_example: The threat of nuclear war kept the major superpowers at peace because they both believed in the MAD doctrine and did everything possible to avoid direct war between themselves.

required_elements: The practical impossibility of a direct nuclear war between the United States and the Soviet Union led the two superpowers to engage in proxy wars around the world instead.
alternatives.correct_example: The practical impossibility of a direct nuclear war between the United States and the Soviet Union led the two superpowers to engage in proxy wars around the world instead.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Restating the prompt without providing a defensible response.
Providing an example not relevant to the specific period or region asked.
common_traps: Identifies a person or event without explaining its historical relevance.
Provides multiple examples when only one is needed — focus on quality not quantity.

parts.prompt:
Explain one way the spread of new technologies contributed to increased economic activity
in the twentieth century.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for explaining ONE way the spread of new technologies contributed to increased economic activity in the 20th century. CB-acceptable responses: (1) widespread fossil-fuel use enabled transoceanic cargo shipping, increasing international trade; (2) nuclear-generated power operated new factories and production facilities, expanding electrical grids and productivity; (3) fossil fuels helped the airline industry grow, increasing tourism and hospitality revenues.

sample_responses.response: The spread of containerized cargo shipping (standardized in the 1960s–1970s) combined with cheap bunker fuel transformed international trade by drastically cutting the cost of moving manufactured goods — this enabled the late-20th-century rise of global supply chains in which Asian factories produced for North American and European consumers.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains a specific technological-economic chain (containerization + fossil fuel shipping → global supply chains) matching a CB-listed response.

sample_responses.response: Affordable jet air travel enabled by fossil fuels (widely available from the 1960s) transformed the hospitality and tourism industries into major economic sectors — mass international tourism became a key source of foreign exchange for countries from Spain to Thailand to Mexico.
sample_responses.commentary: Connects a CB-listed technology (fossil-fuel-powered airline industry) to specific economic outcomes (tourism and hospitality growth). CB-aligned.
Too vague; does not identify a specific technology or explain the economic mechanism.

required_elements: Widespread use of fossil fuels enabled the growth of the transoceanic cargo shipping industry, which increased international trade and stimulated economic growth.
alternatives.correct_example: Widespread use of fossil fuels enabled the growth of the transoceanic cargo shipping industry, which increased international trade and stimulated economic growth.

required_elements: Nuclear-generated power was used to operate new factories and production facilities, increasing the capacity of electrical grids and leading to greater economic productivity.
alternatives.correct_example: Nuclear-generated power was used to operate new factories and production facilities, increasing the capacity of electrical grids and leading to greater economic productivity.

required_elements: Fossil fuels helped the airline industry grow as more people traveled for work and leisure, increasing the revenues of hospitality and tourism industries.
alternatives.correct_example: Fossil fuels helped the airline industry grow as more people traveled for work and leisure, increasing the revenues of hospitality and tourism industries.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Restating the prompt without providing a defensible response.
Providing an example not relevant to the specific period or region asked.
common_traps: Identifies a person or event without explaining its historical relevance.
Provides multiple examples when only one is needed — focus on quality not quantity.
